title: How Long, Lord
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 6-9-24 -PM- How Long, Lord - Psalm 6 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Christian's Struggle with Sin
subpoints: Sin is so awful it took God in the flesh dying on the cross to eliminate its eternal consequences for His people
God chose not to eliminate the temporal struggles and consequences of sin in this life — He could have made us immediately perfect, but chose otherwise in His wisdom
Romans 7:18-24: Paul's description of the ongoing war with sin — "O wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?"
The only thought that can lift us from the pit: Romans 7:25 — "I thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord!" There is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus

outline.title: A Prayer for God's Grace, Not the Removal of Discipline (vv. 1-3)
subpoints: Psalm 6 is a parallel to Psalm 38; Psalm 38 makes explicit that David is going through this because of sin in his life
The language of pain and healing is metaphorical — David had sinned and was experiencing the discipline of the Lord designed to drive him to repentance
David does not ask God to remove the rebuke but to do it in mercy rather than in hot displeasure

Two reasons for this: (1) he knows God chastens those He loves — the absence of chastening would show God didn't care; (2) God's wrath is reserved for those who are not His — if God dealt with him in wrath, it would mean David was outside God's people
David's prayers are based entirely on God's character, not his own: 'Have mercy on me, O Lord, for I am weak' — he bases his plea on who God is, not on what David has done
Begging sin annihilates any confidence and assurance that God is our God — that is where David is

outline.title: How Long, Lord? The Answer
subpoints: David's 'how long' relates to God's displeasure with his sin, but we often feel like asking it about other areas: how long will you make me stay in this marriage? How long will you be distant from me? How long will you let my children rebel?
Psalm 13:1: 'How long, O Lord? Will you forget me forever? How long will you hide your face from me?'
The answer to the 'how long' question: as long as it takes for you to be driven to Jesus Christ
Two vital lessons: (1) God never forgets His people — Isaiah 49: 'Can a woman forget a nursing child? Yet I, the Lord, will not forget you'; (2) There is no such thing as instant godliness

outline.title: God's Purposes in Long Providences
subpoints: God does, sometimes, seemingly forget some of His choice servants — Joseph was shut up in unpleasant circumstances for years; Paul himself; David
But during those times, God was building maturity and godly character into these men — it takes years to grow a sturdy oak tree
David was anointed king in his teens but did not take the throne for many years; God developed him as His man through years of running from Saul and living in caves
If God has placed you in a frustrating circumstance and you have racked your brain to find a way out but nothing has worked — hang on; let God do His perfect work in you
Learn to wait on Him; learn to ask how long; learn to hear the answer: as long as it takes for you to grow more like the Lord Jesus Christ

outline.title: Application: Stop Questioning, Look Out, and Obey
subpoints: Let us stop questioning God as He works in our lives; let us stop looking inward for some reason, and look outward
Let God do the work in us; let us obey Him in loving God and loving others

As we do that, God continues to shape us; eventually the question changes from 'how long will I go through this' to 'how long until I see You, because I can't wait to see You face to face — for then I will be like You'
If David could rejoice at the end of Psalm 6 with only partial revelation, how much more can we, who have the entire revelation of God in Christ?

summary: This evening sermon on Psalm 6 addresses the Christian's ongoing struggle with sin and what to do in the seasons of God's discipline and apparent hiddenness. Pastor Lyro begins with Paul's frank description in Romans 7 of the war with sin — a war so exhausting that the only way out of the pit is the truth that there is no condemnation in Christ Jesus. He then turns to Psalm 6, reading it in light of its parallel, Psalm 38, where David explicitly connects his suffering with sin. David does not ask God to remove His rebuke but to conduct it in mercy rather than in wrath, based on two theological insights: God chastens those He loves (Hebrews 12), so the absence of chastening would mean God did not love him; and God's wrath is reserved for those outside His people, so the presence of wrath would mean David was not God's child. The sermon then addresses the 'how long' question — extended in its application from David's specific situation to the whole range of painful circumstances believers face — and offers a searching answer: as long as it takes to be driven to Jesus Christ. Two lessons close the sermon: God never forgets His people (Isaiah 49), and there is no such thing as instant godliness — the same long providences that formed Joseph, Paul, and David are God's method of building mature, Christ-like character. The sermon ends with a call to stop looking inward and questioning, and instead to look outward, obey, and let God's work proceed.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Christian's Struggle with Sin

Sin is an awful, awful thing. It destroys people, destroys families, destroys churches, it grieves God and brings His displeasure upon the sinner. Sin is so awful and so serious that it took God in the flesh dying on the cross to eliminate the eternal consequences of sin for His people. Yet in His wisdom and sovereignty, God chose not to eliminate the temporal struggles and consequences of sin in the lives of His people. God could have chosen to make us immediately perfect upon our salvation, but He chose to do otherwise in His wisdom and sovereignty.

We continue to carry on this struggle in this life with sin, and as Christians our lives are sometimes marked at times by a constant struggle with sin. I find a lot of comfort in Romans 7. Romans 7:18-24: "For I know that in me — that is, in my flesh — nothing good dwells; for to will is present with me, but how to perform what is good, I do not find. For the good that I will to do, I do not do; but the evil I will not do, that I practice. Now if I do what I will not do, it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells in me. I find then a law, that evil is present with me, the one who wills to do good. For I delight in the law of God according to the inward man. But I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind, and bringing me into captivity to the law of sin which is in my members. O wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?"

I find the struggle with sin so powerful and tiring that at times I just want to give up. At times I am overwhelmed by the feeling that there is not one ounce of grace in my body with which to please the Lord — the feeling that no matter what I cannot measure up to what God requires of me.

Paul ends his discourse with the only thought that can take us from the bottom of the pit of the spirit and set our minds right. He says: "I thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord!" And then in Romans 8:1: "There is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus." It is in Christ and Christ alone who can deliver us from sin and please God.

## A Prayer for Mercy, Not the Removal of Discipline (Psalm 6:1-3)

The Psalmist here recognizes that it is not in him to please God. So he throws himself recklessly as it were upon the mercy of God. In verses 1-3 we find a prayer for God's grace. Although sin is not mentioned in these verses, it becomes evident that David is asking for God's mercy as God deals with his sin. We know this when we compare Psalm 6 with Psalm 38. In Psalm 38, David says: "O Lord, do not rebuke me in Your wrath, nor chasten me in Your hot displeasure. For Your arrows pierce me deeply, and Your hand presses me down. There is no soundness in my flesh because of Your anger, nor any health in my bones because of my sin." Same sort of language, but David explicitly says he is going through it because of sin in his life.

Although it sounds like David is talking about being healed from a sickness, he is actually talking about sin — we must understand the language of pain and healing metaphorically here. David had sinned, and he was experiencing the discipline of the Lord, which was geared to drive him to repentance.

Notice that David does not ask God not to bring upon him the consequence or the chastisement for his sin. Rather, he asks God to do so in mercy. In verses 1-2: "O Lord, do not rebuke me in Your anger, nor chasten me in Your hot displeasure. Have mercy on me, O Lord, for I am weak. O Lord, heal me, for my bones are troubled." It is not the rebuke that David is asking God to remove, but His anger. It is not the chastening he wants God to remove, but the doing of it in wrath.

There are two reasons for what David asks here. He knows that God chastens those whom He loves — therefore the absence of chastening would show that God did not care about him. If you can live as a Christian in sin and there is no consequence, it is because God does not love you. David understood that. The Holy Spirit tells us this in Hebrews 12 — even a simple parent does not let a child go without chastening when they need it; how much more will your Father in heaven do that for us? Being chastened by the Lord is not pleasant, but not being chastened is much less pleasant in the eternal scheme of things.

Two: the wrath of God is reserved for those who are not His. David understood that if God dealt with him in wrath, it would be confirmation that he was not God's child. God was never angry at His people — He was angry at Jesus instead of being angry at us.

Notice that David's prayer for grace and mercy is based entirely on God's character, not his own. He is not coming before God saying, "God, look how much I've suffered for you, look how much I deserve." Verse 2: "Have mercy on me, Lord, for I am weak." So I am weak, but You, Lord, are merciful. He pleads for God's mercy, which is an acknowledgment that he does not deserve anything good from God.

In verse 3: "My soul is greatly troubled, but You, O Lord — how long?" His soul is greatly troubled at the prospect of God being displeased with him in the same way He is displeased with His enemies. And he asks: how long is this going to last? How long is this presence of Your apparent anger going to be here? Persisting in sin annihilates any confidence or assurance that God is our God — that is where David is.

## How Long, Lord? The Answer

David's question, "how long," relates to God's apparent displeasure with his sin. But we often feel like asking this question about other areas: How long, Lord, will you make me stay in this marriage? How long, Lord, will you be distant from me? How long, Lord, will you let my children rebel? How long, Lord, will you make me go through life alone?

Psalm 13:1-2: "How long, O Lord? Will You forget me forever? How long will You hide Your face from me? How long shall I take counsel in my soul, having sorrow in my heart daily? How long will my enemy be exalted over me?"

And the answer is this: as long as it takes for you to be driven to Jesus Christ. That is how long it is going to be. How long until whatever contrary providence in your life changes? As long as it takes to drive you to Jesus Christ.

There are two vital lessons to remember at that "how long" stage. One is that God never forgets His people. He is never going to forget you. Isaiah 49 — Zion, the people of God, says: "The Lord has forsaken me, and my Lord has forgotten me." And then the Lord responds: "Can a woman forget a nursing child and not have compassion on the son of her womb? Surely they may forget. Yet I, the Lord, will not forget you." Isaiah 43: "When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they shall not overflow you. When you walk through the fire, you shall not be burned, nor shall the flame scorch you. For I am the Lord your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior."

God does sometimes seemingly forget some of His choice servants — Joseph, Paul, David — all of them were shut up in unpleasant circumstances for years, during which it seemed like God had forgotten them. But do you know what was happening during those times? God was building maturity into those men as they learned to trust Him.

The second lesson is that there is no such thing as instant godliness. We have instant everything in our society, but there is no such thing as instant godliness. David was anointed king in his teens. He had strong faith at that time, as seen in the story of Goliath. But did God put him on the throne when he turned 21? No. 25? No. 28? No. Through all those years of running from Saul and living in caves, David learned to eat upon God — God was developing him as His man, even in times when it seemed like God had abandoned David.

If God has placed you in some frustrating circumstance and you have racked your brain trying to figure a way out, but nothing has worked — and you see the godless prospering while you suffer, and it seems like God is far away from you — hang on. Let God do His perfect work in you. He has not forgotten you. Learn to wait on Him, learn to ask how long, and learn to hear the answer: as long as it takes for you to grow, to be more like the Lord Jesus Christ.

## Application: Look Outward and Obey

Let us stop questioning God as He works in our lives. Let us stop looking inward, trying to find within ourselves some reason for it, and let us look outward. Let God do the work in us and let us obey Him — loving God and loving others.

As we do that, God continues to shape us. And the question will eventually stop being "how long will I go through this?" and will become "how long until I see You, because I can't wait to see You face to face — for then I will be like You." That is where we are heading.

If David could rejoice as he does at the end of this Psalm with only partial knowledge of God's revelation, how much more can we? We have the entire revelation of God in the face of Jesus Christ. So let us stop questioning God as He works in our lives, let God do the work in us, and let us obey Him.

Father, thank you that you never abandon us, you never leave us. I pray that you would be with us in our times of struggle, especially as we struggle with sin. But often our issue is sin, which is glorious because then the solution is repentance and turning to You. Grant us repentance where we need to repent. Grant us to turn to You through Your Son, that we might grow in Him, that we might grow to be like Him, looking to His coming and the life eternal. In Jesus' name. Amen.
